comments.content:
I recently had a natal chart reading done and I was told that I have the chart of a 'witch' and that there's an indication of strong mediumship ability.

Was wondering if anyone had any recommendations on learning mediumship?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Hmm. First, just as you would with a physician, get a second opinion -- that's a serious step to take on the basis of a single reading. If it does seem to be suitable, spend some time visiting mediums and attending seances so you know what you're getting into and are sure you want to do so. If you decide to go ahead, then find an experienced medium to train you -- there are a lot of "tricks of the trade" that you won't learn from books.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: You've talked in the past about the etheric dimensions of sex; what are the etheric dimensions of buggery, such that our ancestors held the act in such deep horror?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: By "buggery," do you mean gay male sex in general, or anal sex in particular? The answer is different depending on what specifically you're asking. (And, no, these are not necessarily equal to each other.)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Anal sex, with either men or women. Basically, the acts punished with death in the Buggery Act of 1533.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Some cultures have surrounded anal sex with strong taboos. Others didn't and don't. Keep in mind that in 1533 Britain was still subject to Christian religious laws that forbade all sexual activities that couldn't result in pregnancy; that was a lingering reaction to the depopulation of the late Roman world, not a reflection of any particular insight into etheric energy.

The etheric body is not particularly influenced by which physical organs are involved in lovemaking; it's the quality and strength of the etheric response which matters.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Thanks, that makes sense, (though is still very strange from a modern perspective.) Out of curiosity, what would the answer have been in the specifically gay case?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The etheric effects of gay and lesbian sex really depend on the relative polarization of the participants' etheric bodies. That's also true of straight sex, of course -- that's why some pairings result in much more satisfying lovemaking than others. In general, though, if you've got two people whose etheric bodies have complementary genders (remember that the etheric body usually, but not always, has a different gender from the physical body), the etheric results of sex will be relatively healthy irrespective of the genders of the physical bodies involved.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
it's the quality and strength of the etheric response which matters.

This seems like an opportunity to ask you about something I've been wondering about for a while...

Some years ago I started a relationship with a someone, and everything was fine, but when it came to the bedroom things just didn't work. I found this person very pleasant to look at, all the parts in the right place, and so on, but it was like there was a certain sense of electricity that just never appeared. It was the first time your comment that "Sex without the etheric level is just friction" ever made sense to me. It was just friction, and not very enjoyable friction. Even when we did manage to make it all the way, there was a sense of "relief" that I was used to feeling afterward that just didn't happen.

The thing was, the other elements of the relationship lined up, and so we decided to keep at it. At a certain point, the situation reversed, and it became possible to have what felt to me like normal sex, including the sense of relief afterward, at least most of the time. During this time my partner's body also changed substantially, becoming larger and more robust.

My question is, does this point to a misalignment on the etheric plane? And if so, does the reversal demonstrate a kind of switch on that plane? Or was it more a matter of an etheric blockage which cleared up over time?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That's a fascinating case study. Yes, the two of you had etheric bodies that didn't polarize with each other, but over time your etheric bodies adapted -- very likely what happened was that the astral polarity worked so well that your partner's etheric body shifted gears and learned to work with yours. (The change in the other person's physical body shows that it was probably their etheric body rather than yours that made the change.) That can happen; the material body is the most resistant to change, and as you go up the planes the bodies become more responsive and more able to adapt.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Thank you! I've wondered for years if that's what had happened.

Is there any easy way to tell which way each of our etheric bodies polarizes?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
It's kind of complex, but one good if embarrassing key is how you feel after too much masturbation. If you feel drained and empty, you probably have a female etheric body (as most men do). If you feel clogged and congested, you probably have a male etheric body (as most women do). Here are two discussions of how the bodies relate to the planes:

https://www.ecosophia.net/on-the-metaphysics-of-sex/
https://www.ecosophia.net/on-magic-manhood-and-masculism/

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: What if you feel both?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Then your etheric body may be intersex. That also happens. There are immense, and immensely complex, variations from person to person in all the bodies.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The idea of intersex sutble bodies hadn't occurred to me, but it fits. It actually seems like it fits all of them. Is there any significance, beyond this fact, if all of my inner plane bodies seem to be intersex?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It might make sexual relationships complex for you, depending on how well the bodies function in either role. Other than that, not really.

replies.title: This subject is fascinating
replies.content:
And leads me to ask--what is going on in the etheric body (or any other planes) when someone has a very strong predilection for kinky sex (or, for that matter, any type of outright sexual deviance)?

This site has got to have the most diverse set of topics per poster than any others on the net.

replies.title: Re: This subject is fascinating
replies.content: Most sexual kinks are astral -- that is to say, emotional -- rather than etheric. Old-fashioned Freudian psychotherapist analyzed them in great detail, to the point that a good therapist could listen to a few details of somebody's early childhood and accurately predict what their kink was. (The wartime US psychological report on Adolf Hitler had some funny stories about that; everybody in the program figured out within minutes of looking at the first part of his bio that he liked to have young ladies defecate on him -- which was apparently the case.)

replies.title: Re: This subject is fascinating
replies.content: I know, you didn't need to know that. I figure it's my little contribution to puncturing the bizarre cult of of Herr Schicklgruber.

replies.title: Re: This subject is fascinating
replies.content: Well, I guess that would have made orgies with the guy particularly awkward...

replies.title: Re: This subject is fascinating
replies.content: The hosts would have to serve lots of stewed prunes beforehand. ;-)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Keep in mind that prior to antibiotics, an STD could be a very dangerous thing. Look up what syphilis does to people who don't get treated sometime for a taste of this. This is part of why many cultures surround sex with such taboos; and why there is a strong correlation between the severity of the STDs in the society and the extent of the taboos.

Anal sex is more likely to result in disease being spread than vaginal or oral sex, because without sufficient lubricant, tearing is very likely; and so cultures where dangerous STDs are more common tend to have more severe restrictions for a mundane physical reason.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Syphilis spreads extremely easily via vaginal sex -- it doesn't require tissue tears to infect. Thus I tend to think that this argument is one of the many attempts to use dubious medical reasoning to back up a moral belief.

That said, if you happen to be Christian, or have some other religious or moral reason to avoid certain kinds of sexual activity, by all means stick to your beliefs -- just don't try, as Nietzsche said, to justify inherited prejudices via bad logic.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I don't have any kind of moral belief here: I just find that as a general rule, human societies do things that make sense, and this seems like a reasonable justification for

I'll need to dig it up (so I don't think I'll have it in time for the end of Magic Monday, but I think I'll have it ready for the open post), but there are scientific papers, most of them published a while back, that looked at this sort of thing and found that a lot of STDs appear to spread more through anal sex (which is much more common in some areas of the world than others), although there were some exceptions. It's also common for the major medical organizations (such as the CDC) to say that HIV is spread most easily through anal sex, and given their ideological commitments push hard in the opposite direction, I believe them on this point.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: "their ideological commitments push hard in the opposite direction" - no, they don't: (while I do believe the effect exists to a degree) the important part is to blame HIV frequency among gays on an anatomical feature they can "be innocent of", as much as possible to the exclusion of the hypothesis that off-other-people's-scale promiscuity might have something to do with it.

comments.title: Caffeine and its effects on magic
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

I was wondering what effects caffeine has on magical practices such as meditation, ritual, and even divination? I understand not to overdo it as such but I am a pretty heavy consumer of coffee, although my biology seems to tolerate stimulants exceptionally well–I observe no noticeable withdrawal effects from abstaining and I can drink five or more cups per day without it interrupting my sleep schedule. I do wonder how it is shaping my consciousness throughout my daily practice (LBRP, Meditation) and whether it is preventing or could prevent progress. I can obviously experiment myself with abstention or reduction but I was wondering given your experience what you have noticed about caffeine, and specifically coffee, and its effects in relation to magic.

Best regards,

Egyptian Vulture

replies.title: Re: Caffeine and its effects on magic
replies.content: I can't speak to coffee, as I can't drink it -- it gives me migraines. Tea, including fairly strong green tea, has been used for millennia in the East by meditating monks -- Zen monks in Japan are particularly known for their tea habit! Ergo, as far as I know, caffeine is pretty safe when it comes to magical or spiritual practices.

replies.title: Re: Caffeine and its effects on magic
replies.content:
Thanks. As a follow-up question(s), do you know of a good resource, something in the way of a compendium, on the various mental, psychological, and even spiritual effects of substances, natural or otherwise? I know of Culpepper's herbal but I wonder if there are texts that apply astrological or other correspondences towards plant alchemy or magic, instead of merely sorting plants by their (physical) medicinal effects.

While plant alchemy is probably farther down the road for my practice, have you heard of instances of caffeine bearing plants being used in spagyrics having been deliberately selected for their stimulant effects?

replies.title: Re: Caffeine and its effects on magic
replies.content:
1) I wish I did. The nearest equivalent is probably my book The Natural Magic Encyclopedia, and that only covers a restricted range of plants.

2) No, but it's certainly something that could be experimented with.

replies.title: Re: Caffeine and its effects on magic
replies.content: Actually you didn't wrote a book with that exact name; what I found was "Natural Magic: Potions & Powers from the Magical Garden" (1st ed.) and "Encyclopedia of Natural Magic" (2nd ed.).

replies.title: Re: Caffeine and its effects on magic
replies.content: It's the same book; I sometimes forget the title Llewellyn saddled it with in the second edition.

replies.title: Re: Caffeine and its effects on magic
replies.content:
The drinking of coffee was, once discovered, much cultivated by Sufis, and regarded as a useful aid to meditation - although I have no idea as to exact quantities.

It might also have been used to stimulate, just as 'dervish whirling was, the particularly dull and stolid peoples of Central Asia - Rumi observed that they needed livening up a bit.

So perhaps useful for those of Anglo-Saxon, Slavic and Germanic descent, less so to Celts and those of the European South? I am only alf serious, of course.....

replies.title: Re: Caffeine and its effects on magic
replies.content: It's worth noting that in my experience as someone who has traveled a lot, and really likes the taste of it, that coffee seems to have much more positive effects where it grows than where it doesn't. If i drink it in (ex) England, I'll get a migraine, become jittery, and have problems with my spiritual practices. If I drink it in Arabia, where it grows, it doesn't and actually helps improve my meditation and imagery during ritual work.

replies.title: Re: Caffeine and its effects on magic
replies.content:
I can’t drink it either and I really like the smell. If they ever put out a coffee-scented air freshener I’ll buy it.

—Princess Cutekitten

replies.title: Re: Caffeine and its effects on magic
replies.content:
I never used to be able to drink coffee because it gave me an acid stomach.
Now I find that getting older has reduced the natural acid level in my stomach, and I'm fine with it.
Davie

replies.title: Re: Caffeine and its effects on magic
replies.content:
I think it's a very personal thing.

I'm fine with tea (I'm English! Of course I'm fine with tea!) but coffee, or any other energy drink, really messes me up - I am very strongly affected by stimulants, as I have a naturally fast metabolism, and it's just too much. Plus, coffee seems to give people bad breath (from experience with colleagues), gave me weird green stains on my teeth when we had an office coffee machine, and the acidity of coffee gave me stomach upsets. Tea gives me none of those things!

Also, at some point last year, I got a very distinct message in my head from nowhere which said "DON'T DRINK COFFEE!" (I wasn't even drinking coffee). I don't know why or what it meant, but it seemed best to heed it.

Mr. Crow

replies.title: Re: Caffeine and its effects on magic
replies.content:
I take moderate dosages of methylphenidate (ritalin) a stimulant which is much stronger than caffeine, as nothing else really works for my ADHD. I find I can actually focus for meditation and mild magical practice, but I won't attempt anything serious until I resolve some long standing emotional disturbances.

For me ritalin is like coffee, but without the jitters, and I don't think straight stimulants have a major effect on magical practice.

replies.title: Re: Caffeine and its effects on magic
replies.content: If I can chime in, the only thing I've noticed with caffeine and magic is that gradually I couldn't drink coffee more than sparingly anymore, it makes me feel queasy and anxious very easily and that's also true of some over the counter drugs, specially the ones that act on the nervous system though I have no idea if it is related to the practices or something else.

replies.title: Re: Caffeine and its effects on magic
replies.content: You mean gradually as you progressed in your practices? That's an interesting anecdote and it doesn't surprise me that the increased sensitivity and energy gained through magical practice might require the body to take in less artificial outside chemical or nervous stimulation.

replies.title: Re: Caffeine and its effects on magic
replies.content:
I most likely have ADHD of some kind. I never took medication for it... but I did start drinking coffee in junior high. I also like tea -black, oolong, green. I wake up very early, and the morning is the time when I have the most privacy for my practices after my wife goes to work. The caffeine helps. It also helps my concentration and I've come to think that's why I kept drinking it after starting on it so young.

In nomine Maat,

Justin Patrick Moore

replies.title: Re: Caffeine and its effects on magic
replies.content: I apparently have some ADD there in the mix of my neurological issues; when I'm wired late at night and have trouble winding down for sleep, a cup or two of strong green tea will have me relaxed and yawning in no time.

replies.title: Re: Caffeine and its effects on magic
replies.content:
Interesting. Sometimes it doesn't keep me up late... but I often have trouble winding down for sleep. I never thought of it as possibly related to ADHD though. I didn't actually have a diagnosis...It was just a matter of putting two and two together for self-diagnosis. That said, I know that testing isn't always accurate and they have done more studies since then. I figure I dodged a bullet by not medicating. My swirling mind does keep me up more often than I'd like. I look forward to a time when I can be more of a night owl when I want to.

comments.title: Redirecting energies for the benefit of the community
comments.content:
Hi John and fellow commentariat participants--
You mentioned several times in the past that spiritual ministries often run on the rocks because their leaders do not know how to direct the spiritual energies to their god(s) for the blessing of the community; Instead, the energies too often find grounding in sexual scandals--
Could you describe one or more ways to pass such energies along, properly?

My prayers include requesting that I would be a conduit for gods' grace, mercy, peace and healing to all I meet, and I have been praying this twice a day for more than a year. There is a noticeable effect on people around me, to the point that even people who pass by me in the grocery store who I don't know often break out in smiles and do a double-take. It is very strange, because I don't in myself feel any different or blessed, just normal. That's the good part.

The downside, possibly; I have been getting subtle and not-so-subtle signals from some people that they would like to sleep with me. I am not at all in the market for that, and IMHO such activities would likely be harmful to the good works I am currently doing. So--

Is there a ritual or traditional prayer that would help redirect the energies directed at me to blessings for the community? If possible, something that already has worn some tracks in space...
Thanks for hosting this discussion list, and thanks to all who comment here!

replies.title: Re: Redirecting energies for the benefit of the community
replies.content:
1) The traditional method is a little complex but it works well. Basically, a priest or minister has energy flowing toward them from the members of the congregation, and there's a very distinct sexual element to that energy -- that's why Christian churches traditionally have male priests and the core of the congregation is the church ladies, sexually unsatisfied women of a certain age. They pour their unused sex force on the priest, and the priest gathers it up, adds his own love and devotion (and unused sex force), and directs that to God. God responds with a current of astral blessing, which the priest receives and then directs out onto the congregation.

The technical methods here are very simple -- prayer and visualization are usually quite adequate -- but you need to know what's happening and consciously receive and redirect the twin currents. It's when they get stuck in the priest or minister that sex scandals become unavoidable. It sounds to me, though, as though you're at very little risk of that, since you're already doing a basic version of the necessary work.

2) That's absolutely standard, especially these days, when most people have no idea that they can do anything with sex forces but let them flow into the usual channels. Just recognize that they're giving you a current of unused force, and pass that force onto the god you worship. Again, plain prayer and visualization are quite adequate.

When you pray, call to mind a traditional image or symbol of your god, imagining it above and in front of you, and of course ask forgiveness because (as any human image or symbol must be) it is inadequate to represent the divine reality. Then imagine the currents of etheric force streaming toward you from the people in question; see and feel yourself gathering that force up and offering it as a gift to your god, to do with as he wills. Add any of your own unused sex force while you're at it. You may or may not sense the return flow -- don't visualize it, just let what happens, happen. It may come on a plane you cannot yet perceive. Do this regularly and watch things unfold.

replies.title: Re: Redirecting energies for the benefit of the community
replies.content: 1) Didn't Herr Moustache Man utilize that same method? Sans the God part of course. IIRC you mentioned a few times how he would pack sexually-frustrated middle aged women in the front rows and feed off their energy while he was giving his speeches. How does the process work when there is no deity involved? I don't doubt for one minute that the Austrian painter genuinely adored his volk, but wouldn't his case be another example of the sexual energy getting stuck in the "priest"?

replies.title: Re: Redirecting energies for the benefit of the community
replies.content: Yes, Hitler used this method, and in fact I discuss that in my forthcoming book on polarity magic. Don't assume that there wasn't a deity involved -- or, shall we say, a supernatural being of some kind. Hitler didn't show any signs of the kind of frantic sexual excess that you get in people who become dumping grounds for unused sex forces; he was putting the energies to work, and doubtless had help with that process in the Unseen.

replies.title: Re: Redirecting energies for the benefit of the community
replies.content: Huh. This makes a weird amount of sense of the fact that so much of the world since the end of the Second World War has in some ways acted as if they had a massive crush on Hitler....

replies.title: Re: Redirecting energies for the benefit of the community
replies.content: Doesn't it, though?

replies.title: Re: Redirecting energies for the benefit of the community
replies.content:
I've tried the same thing just recently, you know, if I'm in a public place I'll just randomly pray for people around me. And I've been astonished that sometimes I'll look over and someone will be smiling at me. I can't remember this EVER happening before!
Davie

replies.title: Re: Redirecting energies for the benefit of the community
replies.content: If you attune yourself with positive energy -- and that's what prayer does, when it's done with the right intentions -- other people will sense it, and respond positively. It really does make life better for everyone!

replies.title: Re: Redirecting energies for the benefit of the community
replies.content: I have to say that, if I get nothing else out of the MOE material, the Blessing Walk alone will have been worth printing it out and reading it. It's an essential element of spiritual work I haven't done, needing to have it pointed out to me.

replies.title: Re: Redirecting energies for the benefit of the community
replies.content: I remember in the Illuminatus trilogy there was a woman who got her self gang ____d on purpose, apparently to absorb all that male energy.  I know of one "spiritual teacher" who is said to use his male followers in the same way.  I fled from that many years ago.

replies.title: Re: Redirecting energies for the benefit of the community
replies.content: There are all kinds of things you can do with sexual energy if you don't mind exploiting other people and potentially burning out your nervous system. Shea and Wilson didn't know enough about the sexual dimensions of occultism to handle that scene realistically. Fortunately there are also safer and more ethical approaches.

replies.title: Re: Redirecting energies for the benefit of the community
replies.content: A long time ago when I was near the age of 20, I had some brutal menstrual cramps while I was on the train home from Chicago.  I didn't have any painkillers, so out of sheer desperation, I focused on using the energy of the men paying attention to me on the train to alleviate the cramps.  It worked and I was able to pull myself together enough to get home without collapsing.  I was a staunch atheist-materialist when this went down.  Funny how something can work even when you don't technically believe in it.

comments.title: A Few Random Thoughts
comments.content:
JMG et al,

I haven't read any of your Fiction, I read a lot in High School and College though, maybe too much, carrying the stuff around with me! At this time I'm looking at a FAT copy of "Jonathan Strange and Mr. Norrell" by Susanna Clarke, lying on a table in my office. I have threatened to read it for a while now. BCE <------------ (You are Here!) ----------> You May get Here?

LINEAR TIME for the SOUL Obviously Doesn't Exist. The Perception" isn't the Same as it is for the Myriad Personas" on This Vibratory Level (PLANE), so...

The "Linear Filter" does a nice job of isolating all of us "Personas" from each other, but Not from our SOUL! Linearly, we perceive these "Other" Selves as "Past, Future", etc, While Focused in each one.

It goes without saying that this is a Drastic Over Simplification, and is Interwoven with Other Higher Selves / Souls all Aware of Each Other.

This overall concept was sorta, kinda, maybe instrumental in helping to launch the so-called "New Age". It Almost makes TOO MUCH Sense? It's loosely based on "The Nature of Personal Reality", ala, Jane Roberts.

All I've got to add to this is that "IF" Jane Roberts Consciously did in fact "MAKE All THIS UP", I need to scan my Perception: "Right Where I'm Sitting NOW", ala RAW!

comments.title: The Christian Egregore
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

You've written before about the decline of the Christian egegore - a growing sense among many people participating in Christian worship in the last few hundred years that they were "looking into an empty place where God was supposed to be."

I've read accounts from people who have had encounters with Baldr where he told them that he had been leading the descendants of his worshipers in the guise of Christ. C.S. Lewis also reported spiritual experiences with the Greek pantheon that gave him the impression they were a "subspecies" of Christ.

If some of Europe's polytheistic gods maintained contact with devotees through a Christian seeming, would it make sense that, starting with the Renaissance when the first hints of polytheistic devotion began to return to Europe, the gods would start to drop their Christian guise and commune with people calling them by their proper names, thus leading to a falloff in contact with people who had been interacting with them through a Christian veil? Could that be a reason behind the perceived decline of the Christian egregore?

Thanks for hosting these.

replies.title: Re: The Christian Egregore
replies.content: That's a fascinating theory, and it could well be one of the factors involved.

replies.title: Re: The Christian Egregore
replies.content:
I'd love to hear more about these reported encounters. In general, I'm a sucker for stories of interactions between people and gods.

From the many accounts I have read, it makes a lot of sense that the Gods and Goddesses didn't just up and disappear after the religious climate in the West shifted toward Monotheism. They just rebranded themselves as the various saints and angelic entities of those traditions. The idea that the Virgin Mary is simply Isis in a slightly different costume doesn't seem all that far-fetched. Baldr as Christ seems to be just as plausible. BTW, the popular Western depiction of Jesus as a Nordic-looking hippie might play into this just a bit. Finally, I think someone last week posted a link to an article which made a good case for St. Michael being a rather obvious rebadge of Baal Reseph (Nergal); I have a hunch that when modern pagans invoke "Thor" (and similar thunder gods) they're probably calling on this entity, or at least the same energy.

In all likelihood, the Gods wear many masks.

replies.title: Re: The Christian Egregore
replies.content:

When I lived in the Southwest, I could feel it so strongly that I thought, if I ever went (or had to pretend to be) Christian, I'd go with Guadalupe instantly. The only male figure I've ever felt that way about is Francis of Assisi.

The Grey Badger

replies.content:

replies.title: Re: The Christian Egregore
replies.content:
Specifically, I'm thinking of a prayer in Greek from the Orthodox tradition, that is part of their standard opening prayer sequence.

Here it is in phonetic Greek - pardon my spelling because I don't know Greek.

Agios O'Theos
Agios Ischyros
Agios Athanatos
Eleison Imas.

In English -
Holy God
Holy Mighty
Holy Immortal
Have mercy on us.

I find it to be powerful said repeatedly and very slowly, and I find that saying the Greek hits an additional depth.

In the Roman church it is one of two prayers that is still in the liturgy in the original Greek, in the Good Friday service. (The other Greek prayer is, Kyrie Eleison, Lord have Mercy.)

Reading through old Orthodox prayer books I suspect a lot of their liturgy has pre-Christian Greek roots, but it's woven in with the entire liturgy so it's not easy to pull out whole pieces.

https://www.johnsanidopoulos.com/2010/09/miracle-of-trisagion-thrice-holy-hymn.html

replies.title: Re: The Christian Egregore
replies.content:
Something that just hit me, reading this:

With the Renaissance also came the Protestant Reformation. Protestantism, by and large, rejects the saints - they exist, but you're not supposed to worship them or pray to them; you're only supposed to pray to the Trinity. I'm wondering if that also plays into it.

replies.title: Re: The Christian Egregore
replies.content:
This is such a huge topic, of influences between traditional ancient religions and the more recent overlays of religions-of-the-Books or religious founding figures. This interplay has happened not only in Christianity but in Islam and Buddhism as well--any of the religions that emphasize conversion and missionary work. Also the syncretism of the late Pagan Roman religion which adopted many Deities from other religions. I agree that's a very interesting question about what happens to the egregores of all the religions involved in this practice. There are often hybrid religions that result from this, like the religions of the African diaspora: Voudoun, Santeria, etc, with their Christian saints overlaying the loa, which presumably develop their own egregores.

But specifically with (western) Christianity, among the interesting studies is one by sociologist of religion James C. Russell called "The Germanization of Early Medieval Christianity." His thesis is that the Catholic church had to engage in so much spin-doctoring of Christianity and retelling, repurposing, and co-optation of Germanic Heathen beliefs and practices into Christianity, in order to make Christianity palatable and understandable and persuade Heathens to convert, that the Christianity coming out of that process was as much 'germanized' as Heathenism had been Christianized. Christianity was a different animal after going through the evolutions it did in order to appeal to, or successfully be forced upon, Germanic Heathens.

I think this speaks exactly to your question of the effects on the Christian egregore(-s, plural; there must be multiple ones at this point). And it works both ways: for example, the idea among the romantic-era apologists for Heathen religion that Christ and Balder were much the same resulted in scholarly depictions of Balder that are unlikely to reflect his Heathen reality very well, thus changing both the Christian and the Heathen egregore.

Winifred Hodge Rose

replies.title: Re: The Christian Egregore
replies.content:
Hello everybody,

But wouldn't we be relating to archetypes and their respective masks that change according to time, place and context?

My question is: how to achieve this true archetype?

replies.title: Re: The Christian Egregore
replies.content:
Thank you for this! Not long ago, I thought I'd ask Jesus if there were any problems with my migration several decades ago out of Protestant, then Catholic Christianity, onward to Buddhism, and a series of other heartfelt at the time years-long experiments ending at last in my 50's with a sense of being reeled in by the Norse pantheon. The answer from Jesus was faster than I can think, so I'm inclined to call it real: The Jesus in my mind responded with something very much like "I kept you until it was time to pass you along."

There is so much I do not and will not understand in this life. Experiences like this make it all okay.

OtterGirl

PS: I bought and read your book, Heathen Soul Lore Foundations, when JMG reviewed it here on his website. Thank you for writing it! It was most helpful.

comments.title: The Five Rites in ritual setting: a prototype of a magical protocol for health and well-being
comments.content:
I attended last weekend a workshop about magical protocols by the Fraternidad del Círculo Dorado in Guadalajara and to my surprise someone had been using the five rites with an impressive success for some years now. He tells the story of how he thinks this helped to put his wife's cancer into remission using a fairly intensive version of it. Of course. this is not medical advice nor should anyone stop or avoid treatment for such a serious disease but it seems to indicate the effects it can have on spiritual health.

This is also for informational purposes only, as I can't give away their temple methods nor have I tried it extensively, but here is one version I adapted using the Ovate Temple formula from the Celtic Golden Dawn. Their full version invokes the elements, the archangels, the elemental kings and the four divine beasts. This is simpler and seems to work well.

1) Prayer: Guided by (Patron deity or healing deity e.g Sul of the healing springs) I call upon my guardians and ask them for their company and guidance during my days so that with their power they put to rest the storms that might follow me as I try to accomplish my will under the light of the OIW.

2) Perform the full temple opening

3) Perform relaxation and pore breathing using the colorless light

4) Say: "I put my body, emotions and intellect on the hands of my divine will, may it follow the guidance of (patron deity) to restore, balance and enliven me in the Three Circles of existence."

5) Perform the Rites on this space, physically or astrally up to 22 times. This should be worked to, not attempted in the first try. JMG's book The Secret of The Five Rites goes into this in detail.

6) Once finished, you can remain in contemplation, meditation or in active listening for as long as it is helpful for you, seated back on the chair or standing.

7) "With this work, I propose to build my health and well-being by integrating the forces thus called and reflect on the things that I need to give up to maintain it (you can name them if you wish, such as: inertia, addiction, black magic, guilt, fear, anger, shame, etc) and I open myself to (love, light, joy, prosperity, peace, wisdom, etc) "

8) Perform full temple closing

I can say that this packs quite the punch and should by approached with due care. Do not attempt this without building up to it, both magically and physically.

replies.title: Re: The Five Rites in ritual setting: a prototype of a magical protocol for health and well-being
replies.content: Fascinating. I wonder if they got the idea from Riley Crabb of Borderland Sciences Research Institute; in a set of lessons ("The Invisible Reality Behind Appearances") published in 1965, Crabb taught a system of practice in which the Five Rites were integrated with Golden Dawm magic, and students contemplated the ten spheres, archangels choirs of angels, and so on while doing ten repetitions of each of the Rites.

comments.title: Morphine & Karma
comments.content:
Hi JMG,
I am in the process of getting my end of life documents in order. As I have embraced many of the beliefs here about karma since I started reading this blog about 6 months ago, I have begun to wonder about the use of morphine at the end of life to make people “comfortable.” I want to be clear in my instructions to others what I want for myself particularly if I lack mental capacity at the end.

I recall reading recently (was it here?) that morphine interferes with someone’s natural dying process as they begin to cross over while still in their material body on the material plane. It seems that what some people call hallucinations are not actually that but rather part of the transition out of the material body. Does being numbed by morphine make exiting the body and returning to a purely energetic state more difficult for the person? What are the karmic consequences of being numbed by morphine to that process for the person who is dying?

I am also curious about the karmic consequences for family members who choose morphine for a loved one. I am specifically thinking about an auntie of mine who had severe COPD who was clearly dying and suffocating in the liquids related to the decay of her lung tissue. She was given morphine in order to die more peacefully rather than suffocating to death. I do not know if her family got her consent before the drug was administered. I believe she was in hospice care.

Thanks in advance for your wisdom and beliefs about this. It will help me make informed choices for myself as I embrace the occult path.

Along those lines thank you for your thoughtful words to Furnax last week: “Relax; you don't have to learn all of it right away, or at all. You can take it easy, dip your feet into the water a little at a time, wade around in the shallow end for a while -- there's no rush. You are taking your first steps into a world in which you rightfully belong, and day by day, year by year, and life by life, you will come to know more and more of it.”

I rest somewhere in the muddle of excitement and overwhelm with a strong lean towards excitement at all there is to explore and learn and grow into. I feel surprisingly patient about it as I dip my toe in.

Thank you.

replies.title: Re: Morphine & Karma
replies.content: I don't think there's anything wrong with using drugs to treat severe pain in the dying, any more than there's anything wrong with doing other comfort measures in that situation. Morphine numbs the nervous system -- it doesn't affect anything outside the physical plane -- and since you'll be in the process of leaving the body anyway, a little extra help to which you've already given consent shouldn't be any kind of issue.

replies.title: Re: Morphine & Karma
replies.content: Thank you for this.  Very helpful.

replies.title: Re: Morphine & Karma
replies.content:
A personal anecdote:
My grandmother lived to over 100. She was extremely sensitive to opiates of any kind. I was too young to ask much about it, but it seems like they gave her "visions."

I remember visiting her in the nursing home not too long before she died. She told me that she'd recently seen her deceased husband and her youngest daughter (also deceased) after being given morphine for pain.

"They were standing right there at the foot of the bed, beckoning and saying, 'Come on, why are you taking so long? We've been waiting for you.'"

She was a deeply convinced Christian, and thus didn't attribute much significance to this vision. She tried to dismiss it as a foolish side effect of the morphine, as a matter of fact. I wonder, though, if some part of her considered that it was more. She clearly thought enough of it to share the story with me, after all....

*Ochre Harebrained Curmudgeon*

comments.title: Family curse?

Dear JMG, good day to you from England

I follow your writing and have increasingly been interested in the spiritual and occult side of understanding this life and the many links beyond the material. The biggest wake up call however was listening to Jerry Marzinsky (his practice influenced by Swedenborg amongst others, https://www.jerrymarzinsky.com/) in 2021 talk about 'the voices', schizophrenia & the mental health industry. I was a general care worker for 15 years in elderly, learning disabled, autism spectrum, end of life, emergency response, mental health rehabilitation and social therapeutic horticulture. During this time I witnessed the best, the worst, and everything inbetween from fellow care workers, doctors, experts: almost everything was treated with medication, especially the mental health side of things. Watching how it all worked I knew medication solved nothing, but in some cases at least made communication possible (in most others, especially one young man I worked for whose eyes I will always remember, the medication was just sedation to make everyone elses life easier) listening to Marzinsky at the same time as the mandates started in the UK I had to leave care at that point behind as I could not care behind plastic masks, gloves and was never going to take whatever was in those computer mixed and filled vials offered by big pharma

The reason I am elaborating on the above is because on my mothers side she had 9 siblings, 2 heavily touched with voices, both became part of the mental health merry-go-round heavily sedated with medications which did not make the voices go away. But it's not just the voices, all her siblings struggled with severe temper, anger, aggression, drink, addictions and a stubbornness of 'I'm right, you are wrong' without hearing another opinion out (I think more due to said addictions). comments.title: TSW for Getting Books Published
comments.content:
Regular readers of John Michael Greer's other blog may have already seen my announcement on last weekes Ecosophia.net post that I got my first book published, and it is now available for pre-order.

The details are here for those who would like to pick up a copy. Anyone who pre-order's before March 14 will have their name listed in the book as an early supporter, and I thank everyone who does so.

https://velocitypress.uk/product/radio-phonics-laboratory-book/

I finished the final drafts for my book, The Radio Phonics Laboratory, last spring and had started looking for a publisher soon thereafter without much luck. I had made a list of potential publishers, but my subject matter was rather niche. The subtitle "telecommunications, speech synthesis, and the birth of electronic music" put it into a tighter spot for finding a publisher as it focuses on the history of telegraphy, radio, telephone and the role early radio stations and places like Bell Laboratories had in giving birth to the genre of electronic music. The role of speech research is a major thread in this book which makes it quite niche. (Students of the GSF who happen to read this will also find scattered throughout many threads that connect the music I talk about to Pythagorean traditions.)

Having heard nothing from the queries and proposals I'd sent out by last fall I was starting to feel a sense of despair that my dream of getting a book published would not yet bear fruit. Prayer has always been a big part of my life and so, one day I decided I would pray about the matter in an open lodge of the GSF.

Quite soon after that time of prayer in the open lodge I was back in the catalog department at the public library where I work and I noted some of the new books that had just been delivered and that I would be cataloging that day. Among the titles were several about techno music and one that really caught my eye, "Out of Space: How UK Cities Shaped Rave Culture" by Jim Ottewill. I noted the publisher, Velocity Press and looked them up straight away. I was delighted to note they accepted book proposals. I crafted my book proposal for this publisher and on the next available Wed. at the hour of Mercury sent in the proposal.

A few days later I was contacted back with the request to send in a sample chapter. The publisher said my book was niche, but they focusued specifically on electronic music, and thought it might be viable. Would I send in a sample chapter? I dutifully obliged.

Some more time passed and he liked what I sent, and we set up a meeting. He liked what I had sent and wanted to publish my book, and thought there would be a market for it if we could keep the cost down. The work needed to be loosened up with the help of an editor.

Contracts were sent and with a bit of negotiating on my part, I finally got it to reasonable for my status as a first time book writer and signed. I also used astrological timing for negotiating the contract.

This publisher had not at all been on my radar. I am reasonably sure the books were asked to be bought for the library by a patron, and if I had taken the day off, or if one of the three others who do the same work as me had picked the bin that had those books in it, is possible I might not have seen them at all. It was a matter of being in the right place at the right time and an answer to my prayers of finding the right home for the book.


There were a number of synchronicities that showed up. One was a book that I saw at work right when I got the email that the publisher had asked me for a meeting. It was titled "Knock knock, open wide"

Another sign was when I got the contract there was a book that I then arrived on my desk at work called "Shamanic Egyptian Astrology: Your Planetary Relationship to the Gods" by Linda Star Wolf. Not the kind of book I would normally read (a bit on the new age spectrum), but there was a a chapter called "Perfect Timing" in the book that seemed to sum up how things had gone with getting this into the hands of Velocity Press.

I also will note that since my book deals with subjects under the auspice of Mercury, and fast movement, Velocity Press has been perfect and everything has moved with swift velocity since I sent in the proposal. It has been less than six months since that time and the book is already available for people to pre-order.

So TSW!

Thanks JMG for all the magical and spiritual advice here. Wishing all the others who hang out on this forum, and its host, continued success in their work.

Justin Patrick Moore

comments.title: Interesting dream
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I know you don't do dream interpretation, so this is just an interesting story to share.

Last week, I was reading Magic Monday (on Tuesday) and then read a load of stuff about the Irish gods and the proto-indo-european tradition of young men forming warbands (https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/*K%C3%B3ryos).

That night, I had a very strange dream, the kind where you wake up suddenly afterwards and think "I need to write this down". It went as follows:

I was in an 'observer' position, not actually taking part in the events. It was a spring day, and 3 young men went into a forest, which was known to be forbidden, to go hunting. 2 of them are brothers, and they all have Irish accents and are dressed in approximately 1800s clothing and are late teens/early 20s in age. They are laughing and joking, and eventually spot a rabbit, or possibly a hare. They give chase, flanking it, and eventually corner it next to a tree. One raises a slingshot to shoot the creature, when suddenly it transforms into a 'sexy' anthropomorphic rabbit, in the style of Jessica Rabbit from the film "Who framed Roger Rabbit?", but naked. She offers to have sex with the boys if they will agree to let her go free, and also to tell her a secret. She has a playful, lilting, sing-song voice that is somewhere between a Scottish and Irish accent.

I then 'heard' the words "And spill your deepest secrets to her you shall, for she is Lorna MacTabbit, heavenly ho-stess of the Wild Feast" (the ho-stess had a deliberate pause, I think in order to make word play on the word "ho").

The young men excitedly agree, and each of them whisper something into her long ears. Then, the rabbit beckons the older brother forward and she whispers something back into his ear. He flushes red and looks with rage at his younger brother, who steps backward and trips over a tree root. The older brother runs forward and starts beating him on the ground.
Lorna gives a loud, mocking laugh, and transforms back into a rabbit, then sprints into the undergrowth. The other boy leaves the brothers fighting and runs back to his village.

A day later, the brothers haven't returned. The boy sneaks off back into the woods to find them, but can't find a trace of them. He returns to his village in the evening, to find it has been devastated by raiders - the wooden homes and thatched roofs are on fire, and the bodies of his fellow villagers are everywhere. Crows are feasting on the bodies and they all turn to look at him, and let out loud, mocking caws, like laughter.
Then I woke up.

There's a fair bit to unpack from this dream, but from some further research:
- "Lorna" as a name comes from a place called Lorne in Scotland.
- A 'Tabbit' is not a real thing, aside from a tab-paying smartphone app.
- The "Wild Feast" also doesn't appear to exist, but obviously references the Wild Hunt.

The implication was very much that revealing the escaped boy's secret led to the destruction of his village somehow (presumably a word whisphered in the right ear about undefended areas, or valuable loot), and the Wild Feast, instead of the sexual 'feast' initially implied, was actually the feasting of the crows at the end. I didn't get any real sense of malevolence from the dream, but there was certainly a sense of something god-like, and definitely female. The Morrigan obviously springs to mind, with the crows, and the violent punishment for transgression. The whole thing had a very "mythical" feel about it, and very much fits in with mythical tropes. I also had a weird set of dreams/visions for a few weeks in the hypnogogic state as I was going to sleep at the end of last year which were all related to feasting crows, but those were very male feeling instead. I'd been listening to a viking metal song called "Cry of the blackbirds" by Amon Amarth earlier in the evening, so that probably affected things as well. Perhaps this is what happens when one identifies with crows too much...

Any thoughts from others welcome.

Thanks,
Mr. Crow

replies.title: Re: Interesting dream
replies.content:
Umm, not meaning to be picky, but "Eostre" is not Irish nor Celtic, she is Anglo-Saxon; the month-name of Eostre comes from the Old English calendar, while Ostara is from Old High German. She is part of the Germanic Heathen pantheons, not the Celtic. It's astounding how far that misinformation has spread on the internet--that Eostre / Ostara is Irish-Celtic, which I think disrespects both pantheons. Wouldn't the Irish-Celtic spring Goddess be Brigid? She should not be displaced by a Goddess from another pantheon, nor should Eostre / Ostara be displaced; they should each have their proper place in their own pantheons.

The very recently updated Wikipedia entry on Eostre has a great deal of information, including more evidence in favor of Eostre being a genuine Germanic Goddess, not just an invention of the Venerable Bede as some older scholarship has claimed. Evidence ranges from Matronae names of the Roman period in Germany, to personal and month names in a large number of the Germanic languages and dialects across widespread places and times.

Winifred Hodge Rose

comments.title: Internal Family Systems therapy from an occult lens
comments.content:
Have you heard of a form of therapy called Internal Family Systems, invented by one Richard Schwartz? I'm finding myself wondering if Dr. Schwartz was not involved with some sort of occultism at some point-- one of the exercises in his book "No Bad Parts" is pretty much a pathworking.

If you aren't familiar with IFS, the gist of the system is that most of us are not acting as a unified will (again, sounds familiar). This is reflected by sayings like "part of me thinks X, but part of me really wants Y." IFS treats the parts like sub-personalities with their own motivations and (crucially!) holding on to their own trauma (which is, of course, your trauma). There is a core "Self" (inspired by the higher self, perhaps) that these traumatized parts are keeping you from being in touch with, and the goal of therapy is to get you into the Self and use that to help you regulate these subpersonalities and get them to give up their traumas and grudges against you through Love and Understanding.

From an occult standpoint, does any of this seem familiar, and can you comment on how this approach looks to you through that lens?

Thanks.

comments.title: Mouse Utopia
comments.content: I read your article last week on the other blog and wondered: do you have any thoughts on Dr. John B. Calhoun's Universe 25 experiment (aka Mouse Utopia) and it's seeming parallels with what is going on with today's urban population. Is there a magical explanation for what is happening?

replies.title: Re: Mouse Utopia
replies.content: I'm familiar with the experiments, of course. That I know of, it doesn't have a magical explanation, but then it doesn't need one. Living things -- mice and human beings among them -- evolve in certain habitats, and their minds and behavior depend on influences from those habitats just as much as their bodies do. (The field of ecopsychology has explored some of this.) Put any living thing into a sufficiently unnatural habitat, especially a very crowded one, and even the most basic behavior patterns start breaking down. Big cities are a good example: crowded, artificial, and full of highly unnatural stimuli, they generate dysfunctional behavior patterns in an accelerating spiral until they collapse.

replies.title: Re: Mouse Utopia
replies.content: I've been saying this for ages, that cities can make people crazy.

replies.title: Re: Mouse Utopia
replies.content:
Some critics are quick to point out that we're not rodents, and that's true, but there's an analogous lesson for us: enough dysfunctional behavior can cause you to crash and burn, even if you have plenty of resources. It's not just cities, the entire modern Western society is showing signs of serious dysfunction & decay. We're not quite in a behavioral sink, but all is not well, and those experiments are as relevant as ever. And yes, while magic intersects with all things, a particularly magical explanation is not required.

--xcalibur/djs

replies.title: Re: Reincarnation Blues

comments.title: Some definitions
comments.content: What is the difference between a rite, a ritual and a ceremony?

replies.title: Re: Some definitions
replies.content: The same as the difference between a car, an auto, and a four-wheeled motor vehicle.

replies.title: Re: Some definitions
replies.content: One of the distinctions I've heard made, and it involves Rite vs Ceremony, is that a rite is the thing that has the "vertical connection" with the transcendent realm, and the ceremony is what contains it. So, broadly speaking, in a Mass, the Rite is the consecration of the bread and wine, the ceremony is what goes around it. The ceremony may or may not have a subtle effect, but the Rite always does, by definition.

replies.title: Re: Some definitions
replies.content: No question, some people have put various spins on each of these words, but there's no commonly accepted distinction.

replies.title: Re: Some definitions
replies.content:
To add onto this, I think "ritual" emphasizes the literal words, actions, and steps taken, while "rite" emphasizes the transcendent or spiritual element as you said, and "ceremony" encompasses both. But really, these are shades of meaning, the words are similar.

My father was a serious occult practitioner from the 1960s until his death more than a decade ago. Besides his involvement in Western occultism, which went back 2 generations to his grandmother who was a Theosophist heavily involved with a lodge, he also spent years in India devoted to Kali, and at a separate time in his life was in Latin American working with psychedelics and shamans there.

The shaman said to me: “I know your father. I called you here to tell you not to trust him. He tried to have you killed, you know, and you cannot trust him.”

I took the dream seriously enough to cancel a planned visit with my father, and ended up not seeing him before his death. (I have no regrets.)

Years later I learned from a third party that when I was a toddler, he spoke about killing me (or my mother and me) because my mother was trying to get child support from him. I did not know this when I had the dream.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The two outer planets -- Pluto isn't a planet -- are badly named, which is not surprising since they weren't named by priests or mystics. Uranus should probably have been named Prometheus, because it's got that kind of disruptive, rebellious, revolutionary energy. Neptune might better have been named Rhea after one of the greatest of the Titanesses, the older sister of Saturn and mother of the gods. Neptune's energy is very feminine; it is an energy of unity, of the dissolution of all individual things into the waters of the Great Deep. You can invoke Uranus/Prometheus and Neptune/Rhea, if you know your way around the Tree of Life, with the symbolism and correspondences of Chokmah and Binah respectively.

Pluto is a minor body, like Ceres and Eris -- there are probably quite a few others further out in the Kuiper Belt, out past Neptune. My experience with Pluto is that it's primarily about overblown hype, addictive fantasies, and dreams that will never be realized. (Think about the most obsessive popular concerns during the era when Pluto was mistaken for a planet: space travel, nuclear power, Communism, Freudian psychology, and the list goes on -- all of them promised a utopia and failed utterly to deliver it.) I would have named it Hypnos, after the spirit of sleep and dreams. Ceres, on the other hand, was well named -- she governs issues of food, nutrition, and nurturing.

Interestingly, Isabel Hickey, a 20th century American astrologer, predicted several years before Pluto's huge moon Charon was discovered that Pluto was one half of a double planet system, and named the other half Minerva. She held that you could choose whether to be caught up in Plutonian delusion or to accept Minervan wisdom. It's an interesting theory and seems to work.

comments.title: Group Minds and Gender
comments.content:
Towards the end of last week's Magic Monday there was a question regarding the occult dimensions of the trans phenomenon. I am not the OP for that question, but it sat with me for awhile, and I think there might be something more going on than was discussed.

I cannot find the exact post, so it was probably a comment to a post, but in the course of the "magic is not what you want it to be" series on dreamwidth there was a working brought up where people were trying to make their spells hip with currently fashionable political trends by switching their intention of the white and black candles or pillars of their working setup and directing negative energy towards the white candle and positive energy towards the black candle. The commentary was that it is true that any object can be a symbol with whatever intention you want, but just because you change your surface intention, the track in space already associated with the symbol is good and deep and carved by many feet other than the particular mage in question, so the workings done with the symbols reversed have a good chance of getting mixed up and falling prey to the subconscious track in space despite any good intentions.

I've noticed in the gender discourse that it is frequently brought up whether men or women are interchangeable. Some people point to women moving into high-profile office jobs and doing as good as men, while the opposition seems to then go down to the farm and insist that because not everyone can throw 100lb hay bales about all day that there are some fundamental differences that are universally applicable.

Or in other words, a large (thought not necessarily entire) part of the debate centers around work. And certain jobs have been emotionally charged with a lot of energy and focus which was not taken into account as we shuffled around who ought to be doing what from single genders in many roles to mixed genders in many roles. The resulting tracks in space are messing with a lot of people and our society is not well positioned with esoteric knowledge to understand and sort out the problem. In fact, discussing it at all seems very rocky territory and historically men and women seem to make two worlds socially and keep to themselves, so the problem may not be very solvable to begin with.

If a stay at home dad takes his kid to a playground where the stay at home moms are, their group mind needs to figure out what to do with him. Is he a potential mate? A rival? Would allowing him into the group make them appear unfaithful or perhaps risk leaking knowledge women keep to themselves?

If a women enters a formerly all male workplace, the men's group mind now has to figure out is she a colleague? A love interest? If she does better than you at your job, is this just she's a higher man on the totem pole or are you completely emasculated for being beaten "by a girl"? If she solves a problem no one else could, is she a brother in arms?

And if the outsider house husband or first woman CEO is entering those environments, they will have to contend with how they conform to those environments - integration means partaking in the social rituals and patterns of cooperation/competition laid down predominantly by people of the other gender that haven't been reoriented towards a gender neutral context, if such a thing is even possible/desirable. Again, forthright discussion is frequently heated and/or taboo and so much is subconscious that it would take a lot of effort to sort out.

These then land on the third rail of a species needing to gain status, identify mates, and reproduce. If you don't know if that biologically compatible person is a potential mate to be approached or a colleague where your advance would be socially ruining, instinct gets confused, and if reproductive instinct is confused I could see how that might reach down and irritate the collective group mind. For instance, your mention of young men turning to Shemales for their adult entertainment could be a symptom of a difficult to interact with dating scene driving them to said entertainments mixing with the tension between colleague and potential mate driving the subject matter. So I'm wondering if the collective western group mind is now trying to sort out the mixed tracks in space by identifying anyone with the slightest interest in crossing those lines as early as possible and directing them out of the gene pool to clear the floor for courtship?

Since the PMC is in workplaces that have fewer physical restrictions and thus are easier on the face of it to have genders comingle, their group mind is most impacted thus they would be the ones doggedly driving for a resolution to their issues, while I would expect people in work environments more segregated by physical demands to be less impacted because while anyone could in theory work there, in practice workers still tend to be of one gender or the other by choice so the group minds don't clash.

There could also be a divine element in play as well - I'm not sure if Aphrodite has much patience for pronouns if she's decided two people need to hook up. Mythologically, we could say that Hermes is passing between Aphrodite and Ares with the result being a world class mess.

1a) What can someone with the magical chops of a candle spell or red bag do to navigate having to interact with a group mind of predominantly a different gender such that the interaction has the best chance to be neutral or beneficial?
1b) Are there any signs they should keep an eye on with regard to the group mind interacting with this or that subtle body?
1c) If I were in charge of a comingled group or workplace, are there any measures I could take to manage said crossed tracks in space?

2a) If the gender mess has reached up to the divine level, would planetary charity and prayer be enough for at least local results, or are we just going to have to take the lumps?
2b) What spiritual powers might be good to consult about the situation? I'm comfortable with Egyptian and Eclectic Christian pantheons.

replies.title: Re: Group Minds and Gender
replies.content: That's a fascinating suggestion and one I'll want to reflect on. As for your more practical questions, I'd suggest meditation and divination on those, followed by experimentation -- I don't know that anybody has tried to sort things out along the lines that you've sketched out, and experience is the only worthwhile test here.

Last week, there was a discussion of gender dysphoria being the result of remembering a prior life without having had time to process it. This made me wonder why it would be so much more common in the privileged classes, and then something occurred to me: it must take pretty weird karma to be born into what is by any historical standard, a pretty weird situation. The less historically normal, the less ordinary the karma must be for the soul to get there. If the weird situation is also a self-limiting phenomena, then there would also be some intense pressure to be born there while it's possible; and so there will be weird effects on reincarnation that are present in this interval of time, and especially noticeable among whichever class of people are living such unusual lives.

Thus, I don't think it's a coincidence that right now, you have people who can remember overlapping lives; these are possible because time on the inner planes is fluid compared to the physical, but ordinarily don't happen because things get really weird if it does. But, right now, the privileged classes exist under already weird, and historically very unusual circumstances, and ones which allow for some forms of karma to be burned off in ways that otherwise are a lot trickier. They thus serve as a strange attractor, pulling souls towards them, and messing with some of the normal patterns of incarnation, such as the rule the next life must be later than the previous one. Under some circumstances, this appears to be enough to force souls to reincarnate earlier than their most recent death.

This also implies that souls who end up here (such as myself) are far more likely to have had very short gaps between incarnations, and that all of the usual sort of issues with that are most directly shown with these social classes; and that this is why things like gender dysphoria have grown so much more common in this social class than any other.

Does this line of logic make sense to you?

comments.title: Passing onto new realms of existence
comments.content:
﻿﻿Dear JMG
My dear father passed peacefully in his bed about one month ago, and I have two experiences I’d like to share and ask if you have any comments upon.

As I entered my dad’s room to check on him that early morning of his passing, the room most distinctly smelled of flowers. He had very recently died, as his body was still warm. There was a calm expression on his face. (He was 93, on home hospice for heart failure.)

A friend later remarked that when the angels come to help usher out a soul, they leave the scent of flowers! I found that comforting and believable.

The second experience was more odd. After finding him passed and then grieving with his body, I left his room. As I passed a table where some (unused) narcotics were, I felt the most distinct urging to swallow the pills to follow him! I shook it off but it was bizarre. I was very close to my dad, and he was a great loving and teaching father, so it was almost “natural” to feel like following his lead into another dimension, yet I also distinctly felt that the urge was somewhat “external”. It didn’t feel evil, just a kind of energetic pull.

Thinking about it, I concluded that because he had just passed shortly before I found him, strong death forces were still present and I came into their vicinity?

It was certainly an experience that, though peculiar, was not one laden with fear.
Maybe it’s my natal Scorpio moon, but I’m not too freaked out by the death process, yet, of course, do still deeply feel painful mourning for the departed. I am absolutely positive my dad’s soul is at peace and he is with my mom in the heavenly realms, and was honored to have hopefully brought comfort to their passing experience.

Thank you SO much JMG. I am grateful for all that you do for our community.

I know very few GD mages who've done either of those with the patience and persistence they require. Meditate on everything in the knowledge lectures, no matter how simple or obvious it seems; there are layers upon layers of meaning in there. Oh, and you'll need three systems of divination -- tarot, geomancy, and astrology. All three have important roles in GD symbolism.

replies.content: Yes. You basically can't get to Geburah until you've mastered the lessons of Tiphareth, and once you do that crossing via the Path of Teth is easy.
